segments.inferno-20-g34: perché is the interrogative 'why' introducing the question. lasci is the 2nd-person singular present form (tu) of an older verb form (lascare/lasciare) meaning 'leave' or 'abandon'—here used as a simple present question 'do you leave/are you abandoning'. la is the feminine singular definite article 'the' modifying guerra. guerra is a noun 'war' or 'battle'.
segments.inferno-20-g35: This segment is only the sentence-final punctuation (a period) closing the line/question; there are no lexical items to analyse.
segments.inferno-20-g36: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' non restò di ruinare is an impersonal past construction: restò (passato remoto, 3rd sg.) here means 'there remained/was left,' and the particle di introduces the following infinitive ruinare ('to fall down'); together the phrase means 'nothing remained but to fall.' a valle is an adverbial phrase 'down into the valley.' fino a introduces a limit 'as far as' and Minòs is the proper name; che is the relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing the defining clause, ciascheduno 'each/every one' is the subject of the relative clause, and afferra (present indicative 3rd sg.) 'seizes' describes Minos's action (a timeless/present description used in narrative).
segments.inferno-20-g37: Mira is an imperative 'look' or 'behold.' c’ ha fatto is a periphrastic perfect: ha fatto (present perfect, 3rd sg. of avere + past participle fatto) = 'has made.' The clitic c’ is an archaic contracted element (here serving anaphor/relative/locative function) and is not rendered by the provided sense list. petto de le spalle is literal word order 'breast of the shoulders' (de le = delle, archaic form) meaning 'a chest formed from/pushed out by the shoulders.'
segments.inferno-20-g38: perché here functions as 'because' giving the reason. volle is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of volere 'he wanted/wished' and governs the bare infinitive veder ('to see'); troppo davante is an adverbial phrase 'too far ahead' ('troppo' modifying 'davante').
segments.inferno-20-g39: di retro guarda is an idiomatic phrase 'he looks backward' (di retro = 'from/behind/backwards'; guarda = 3rd sg. present 'he looks'). e fa retroso calle = 'and makes a backward/retreating way/path'—fa (3rd sg. present of fare) + complemento 'retroso calle' (retreating path), i.e. 'he turns back' or 'he retreats.' The use of present forms here follows narrative-poetic usage to describe immediate result.
segments.inferno-20-g16: Se is the conditional 'if' (archaic/poetic use). Dio is the subject 'God'. lasci is a 3rd-person present subjunctive (from lasciare) meaning 'allows' or 'lets' and is used after the conditional 'Se' to express 'if God allows'. ti is the indirect object 'you'.
segments.inferno-20-g17: Lettor is the vocative 'reader' (addressing the reader directly); the form is truncated (no final -e) as in Dante's diction.
segments.inferno-20-g18: Prender frutto is an idiom 'to take/derive fruit' = 'to obtain profit or benefit'; prender is the infinitive 'to take/derive' and frutto is its object. Di introduces the source ('from') and tua lezione ('your reading/lesson') is the noun phrase indicating what one might profit from.
segments.inferno-20-g19: Or = 'now' (literary). Pensa is the (imperative or present indicative used as exhortation) 'consider/think'. Per te stesso = 'for yourself' or 'to yourself', reflexive/emphatic phrase. Com' is the contracted form of come 'how'; io potea is the imperfect indicative 'I could' (potea = potevo in modern Italian), an old/poetic form; tener lo viso asciutto literally 'to keep the face dry' where tener = 'keep' and lo viso 'the face' (lo = masculine article used before vowel in older usage), asciutto = 'dry'.
segments.inferno-20-g20: Quando = 'when'. La nostra imagine = 'our image/likeness' (la + adjective nostra + noun imagine). Di presso is a phrase meaning 'close up' or 'nearby' (di + presso). Vidi is the 1st-person singular preterite (past) 'I saw' (Dante spells the infinitive vido/vidi). Sì = 'so' or 'so much', and torta is feminine adjective agreeing with imagine, here meaning 'twisted/distorted' (poetic use).
segments.inferno-20-g21: Che introduces a result clause 'that' / 'so that'. ’L is the elided article (lo) 'the' before pianto 'weeping/tears'. De li occhi = 'of the eyes' (de = di, li = plural article). Le natiche 'the buttocks' is the grammatical subject here; bagnava is the imperfect 'was wetting' / 'wet' (3rd-singular imperfect bagnava) describing the ongoing effect; per lo fesso = 'through the cleft' (per = through, lo fesso = 'the cleft' or 'the split').
segments.inferno-20-g22: Certo = 'certainly' / 'indeed' used as an emphatic adverb. Io is the subject 'I'. Piangea is the imperfect 'I was weeping' (piangea = piangevo in modern Italian); the imperfect conveys continuous past action.
segments.inferno-20-g23: Poggiato is a past participle used predicatively—'leaning' or 'resting'—agreeing implicitly with the subject (io), and is followed by the prepositional phrase a un de' rocchi 'on one of the rock-stumps'. Del duro scoglio = 'of the hard rock' (del = di + il).
segments.inferno-20-g24: Sì che introduces a result clause 'so that'. La mia scorta 'my guide/escort' is the subject of the following verb; mi disse is a simple past (disse) 'said to me'. The whole segment links the previous gesture (leaning and weeping) to the guide's spoken reaction.
segments.inferno-20-g25: Ancor = 'still/yet'. Se’ is an archaic contracted form of sei (second person singular present 'are'); here the guide addresses 'tu' directly: 'Are you...?' Tu is explicit 'you'. De li altri sciocchi = 'of the other fools' (de = di; li altri = the others; sciocchi = noun 'fools' in address). The whole is a direct question.
segments.inferno-20-g26: Qui = 'here' or 'in this matter'. Vive is the present indicative 'lives' (third singular). La pietà = 'pity/compassion' (feminine noun). Quand' è ben morta = 'when it is well dead'—quand' is the contracted quando 'when', è is 'is', ben = 'well', morta is the past participle used adjectivally 'dead' (feminine to agree with pietà). The line is paradoxical/ironic.
segments.inferno-20-g27: Chi = 'who' (introducing a rhetorical question). È più scellerato = 'is more wicked' (comparative). Che colui che = 'than he who'—che here functions as 'than'. Al giudicio divin = 'to/at the divine judgment' (al = a + il; divin = adjective 'divine' in older spelling). Passion comporta = 'carries/introduces passion'—comporta is present indicative 'brings' or 'bears' and passion means 'strong emotion' here. The sentence condemns allowing passion to influence divine judgment.
segments.inferno-20-g28: Drizza is an imperative (second person singular) 'raise' / 'straighten' (drizzare = to straighten, raise). La testa = 'the head' (direct object). The command is repeated for emphasis.
segments.inferno-20-g29: The second drizza is a repetition (imperative) for emphasis—'raise (it)'. The object (la testa) is understood from the previous clause.
segments.inferno-20-g30: E = 'and'. Vedi = imperative or present 'see' (addressing the reader/listener). A cui = 'to whom' (relative/interrogative sense). S’aperse is a reflexive/passive past (si + aperse, 3rd-person singular aorist/past) meaning 'opened to' or 'was opened to'—the clitic s’ marks the pronominal/passive construction. A li occhi d’i Teban la terra = 'the earth opened to the eyes of the Thebans' (a li occhi = 'before the eyes of', d'i = di + i). Word order is literary: verb before subject 'la terra'.
segments.inferno-20-g31: Per ch' = 'wherefore/thereupon/so that' introducing the consequence. Ei is an archaic form for 'they' (third-person plural). Gridavan is the imperfect 'they were shouting' / 'they shouted repeatedly'. Tutti = 'all' (the bystanders). The clause reports the crowd's reaction.
segments.inferno-20-g32: Dove = 'where'. Rui is an archaic 2nd-person singular present of ruggere/ruire? Here it means 'do you rush/go headlong' or 'where are you rushing/falling' addressed to Amphiaraus; the verb is an archaic poetic form. The line is the crowd's exclamation to the sinking/vanishing hero.
segments.inferno-20-g33: Anfïarao is the proper name 'Amphiaraus' called vocatively; the diaeresis marks the pronunciation of the two vowels.
segments.inferno-20-g1: Di nova pena is a prepositional phrase meaning 'because of/of new suffering' (Di = 'of'/'because of'; nova = 'new'; pena = 'suffering'). Mi conven is an impersonal/dative construction with convenere meaning 'to be necessary/fitting for me' (mi = dative 'to me'); the verb is followed by two infinitives, far versi e dar matera, where far (archaic form of fare) + versi = 'to make/write verses' and dar (archaic of dare) + matera = 'to give/provide subject‑matter'. Al ventesimo canto is the indirect object phrase 'to/for the twentieth canto' (al = a + il), and de la prima canzon is a genitive/attributive phrase 'of the first song' (de = dialectal/poetic di; canzon = canzone).
segments.inferno-20-g2: Ch’ è is the clitic form of che + è ('which is'); d’ i sommersi means 'of the submerged' (d' = di 'of', i = the, sommersi = 'the submerged' used as a substantive adjective). The clause is a simple relative identifying the subject‑matter of the cited canto.
segments.inferno-20-g3: Io era già disposto: era is the imperfect of essere ('was'), disposto is a past participle used adjectivally = 'prepared'; già = 'already'. Tutto quanto (lit. 'all that') here intensifies to mean 'entirely' or 'completely'. A + infinitive (a riguardar) marks purpose/readiness: 'prepared to look (riguardar)'. Ne lo scoperto fondo = 'into the uncovered/exposed bottom/depth' where ne + lo functions as 'in/into it, the uncovered depth'.
segments.inferno-20-g4: Che introduces a relative clause modifying fondo. Si bagnava is an imperfect reflexive/inchoative form meaning 'was being bathed/wetted' (si indicates the intransitive/reflexive construction), d’ angoscioso pianto = 'by/with anguished weeping' (d' = di). The imperfect gives the continuous/background action.
segments.inferno-20-g5: E vidi ... venir: vidi is the perfect of vedere ('I saw') and takes an infinitive clause venir to express perceived action (I saw people come). Per lo vallon tondo = 'through the rounded/round valley' (per = through). The noun order is typical: 'vidi gente per lo vallon tondo venir' = 'I saw people coming through the round valley.'
segments.inferno-20-g6: Tacendo e lagrimando are present gerunds ('being silent and weeping') that describe the manner in which the people were coming; they accompany the verb venir and specify simultaneous action.
segments.inferno-20-g7: Al passo che fanno introduces a comparative/relative phrase: al passo = 'at the pace/manner', che fanno = 'that they make/do'. Le letane is the plural substantive (poetic) 'the lethean/forgetful ones' (Lethean or 'forgetful'); in questo mondo = 'in this world'. Together: 'at the pace that the forgetful keep in this world.'
segments.inferno-20-g8: Come introduces a temporal/comparative clause ('as/when'). '’l viso mi scese' literally 'the face/sight descended to me' — here mi is a dative of experience and scese (scendere, p.s.) describes the narrator's gaze moving down; in lor più basso = 'into them, lower down' (in + lor = into them/their midst; più basso = 'lower').
segments.inferno-20-g9: Mirabilmente apparve esser travolto is a periphrastic expression: apparve (it appeared) + infinitive esser + past participle travolto = 'it appeared to be overturned/overwhelmed'. Ciascun is the subject 'each one', and tra 'l mento e 'l principio del casso is the spatial phrase 'between the chin and the beginning of the chest/torso' specifying where they were overturned.
segments.inferno-20-g10: Ché = 'for/since' giving the reason. Da le reni = 'from the loins' (da = from). Era tornato (era + past participle) indicates a past state/result: 'the face had been turned back' (tornato = turned/returned). '’l volto' = 'the face'.
segments.inferno-20-g11: E in dietro venir li convenia: e = 'and'; in dietro = 'backwards'; venir is the infinitive 'to come'; li is a dative/clitic 'to them'; convenia (imperfect of convenire) is used impersonally/datively to mean 'it was necessary/fitting for them' — so the construction expresses 'they had to come/go backwards.'
segments.inferno-20-g12: Perché introduces the reason 'because/since'. '’l veder' is a nominalized infinitive ('the seeing' / 'the power to see'); dinanzi = 'in front/forward'; era lor tolto = 'was taken from them' (era + past participle tolto = 'had been taken away', lor = 'from them' / 'to them').
segments.inferno-20-g13: Forse = 'perhaps'. Per forza here means 'by force' or 'by compulsion/violence' (giving a possible cause). Già = 'already'. Di parlasia is a genitive/qualifying phrase (here 'of speech' / 'by speaking' — the exact nuance is ambiguous but grammatically it's a di + noun phrase). Si travolse is the perfect reflexive 'was swept away' (third‑person singular, travolgere); così = 'thus/so'; alcun del tutto = 'some one completely' (alcun = some/anyone, del tutto = 'entirely/utterly').
segments.inferno-20-g14: Ma io nol vidi: ma = 'but'; io = 'I'; nol is the clitic fusion of non + lo = 'not him/it' (direct object negative); vidi is the passato remoto of vedere 'I saw' — literal: 'but I did not see him/it.'
segments.inferno-20-g15: Né credo che sia: né = 'nor'; credo = 'I believe' (present); che introduces a subordinate clause requiring the subjunctive (sia) after a verb of belief/doubt; sia is the present subjunctive of essere = 'that it be/that it is', here expressing the speaker's doubt or disbelief ('nor do I believe that it is so').
segments.inferno-20-g74: Ivi is an adverb meaning 'there'. Convien che is an impersonal phrase 'it is necessary that' and takes a subordinate clause introduced by che. Tutto quanto forms a unit meaning 'everything that/all that', with quanto reinforcing tutto; caschi is the present subjunctive of cascare (used after convien che) meaning 'fall' or 'drop'. Ciò che introduces the relative clause 'that which'; '’n grembo a Benaco star non può' literally 'cannot stay in Benaco's bosom' — grembo is 'bosom/lap', a is the preposition 'at/in' introducing Benaco, star (stare) is an infinitive 'to remain/be located', and non può is the present indicative 'cannot'.
segments.inferno-20-g75: E links this result to what follows ('and'); fassi is an archaic/poetic passive-like or middle form of fare/essere meaning 'become' or 'is made' (here 'becomes'); fiume is the noun 'river'; giù is an adverb 'down'; per indicates motion 'through/across'; verdi modifies paschi as 'green' (a pastoral epithet); paschi is the plural 'pastures'. Together: 'and it becomes a river down through green pastures.'
segments.inferno-20-g76: Tosto che is a temporal conjunction 'as soon as'. L' acqua is the subject 'the water'; a + infinitive (a correr) marks the start of an action 'to run' (here 'begin to run'); mette is from mettere and in the periphrasis mettere a + infinitive means 'sets about/puts itself to' (i.e. 'begins'); co is an archaic contraction of con ('with') here functioning as a final particle often attached in older Italian to indicate accompaniment ('with it' or 'along').
segments.inferno-20-g77: Non più here means 'no longer'; Benaco is the name of the lake (Lake Garda). The short clause 'non più Benaco' states that the name no longer applies at this point.
segments.inferno-20-g78: Ma introduces the contrast 'but'; Mencio is the local name (the Mincio). Si chiama is the reflexive/passive construction meaning 'is called' (si + chiamare = 'is named'); fino a Governol means 'as far as/until Governolo' with fino a introducing the extent.
segments.inferno-20-g79: Dove is a relative adverb 'where'; cade is the present indicative 'falls'; in + Po indicates motion into the Po river: 'falls into the Po' (cade in Po).
segments.inferno-20-g80: Non molto ha corso is a compact clause: non molto 'not much', ha corso is the perfect tense of correre ('has run/flowed'). The subject (the stream/river) is implied; the sense is 'it has not run far'.
segments.inferno-20-g81: Ch' (che) introduces a dependent clause often temporal/causal here: 'when/that'; el is a dialectal/poetic form of il used as subject pronoun 'it' (the river); trova is present 'finds'; una lama is 'a lama' — here 'lama' means a marshy pool or shallow marsh.
segments.inferno-20-g82: Ne la qual = 'in which' (ne = in/therein; la qual = the feminine relative 'which' referring to lama). Si distende is a reflexive/middle usage 'spreads itself out' or 'extends'; e la ’mpaluda = 'and makes it marshy/stagnant' where la refers back to the lama and mpaluda (from impaludare) means 'to bog down / make a marsh of'.
segments.inferno-20-g83: E suol di state talor essere grama: suol (from solere) with an infinitive means 'is wont to' or 'usually'; di state = 'in summer'; talor = 'sometimes'; essere grama = 'be wretched/unpleasant/poor' — here describing the condition of that stretch in summer.
segments.inferno-20-g84: Quindi is an adverb meaning 'thence/from there' (or 'then' depending on context). Passando is a present participle 'passing' that modifies the subject la vergine cruda ('the harsh virgin'); vide is the preterite 'saw'; terra is the direct object 'land'.
segments.inferno-20-g85: Nel mezzo del pantano is a locative phrase 'in the middle of the marsh' (nel = in the, mezzo = middle, del pantano = of the marsh). It specifies where the land is located.
segments.inferno-20-g86: Sanza is an archaic form meaning 'without'; coltura is 'cultivation'; e d’abitanti = 'and of inhabitants' (d' = di). Nuda is an adjective 'naked/bare' placed after the nouns for emphasis, meaning 'devoid of cultivation and inhabitants' (i.e. barren and uninhabited).
segments.inferno-20-g63: 'Suso in Italia' is a fronted locative phrase (poetic 'suso' = 'up' or 'above') placing the scene; 'giace' is the 3rd-person singular present of giacere meaning 'lies/is situated'. The adjective 'bella' precedes the verb here for poetical emphasis ('a fair/beautiful'), and 'un laco' is the subject (an indefinite noun phrase 'a lake').
segments.inferno-20-g64: 'a piè de l'Alpe' is the prepositional phrase 'at the foot of the Alp' (a piè di + noun). The relative clause 'che serra Lamagna' ('which encloses Lamagna') uses che as a relative pronoun and serra as 3rd-person singular present of serrare meaning 'encloses/presses together'. 'sovra Tiralli' is a locative modifier placed after Lamagna: 'above the Tyrol'.
segments.inferno-20-g65: c'ha is a contracted/clitic phrase and functions like 'che ha' or colloquially 'ci ha' here: ha is the 3rd-person singular of avere 'has'. The construction 'ha nome Benaco' means 'is named/carries the name Benaco' with the lake understood as the subject.
segments.inferno-20-g66: Per mille fonti is a simple prepositional phrase: per here means 'by' or 'through' and mille modifies the plural noun fonti ('springs'). It functions as an adverbial complement to the verb that follows.
segments.inferno-20-g67: credo is the 1st-person present 'I believe' and is parenthetical here; it softens the assertion that follows. In Dante this is a straightforward present-tense verb.
segments.inferno-20-g68: The clause 'e più si bagna tra ...' means 'and moreover it is bathed/washed between ...' si is the reflexive/impersonal clitic marking that the lake is acted upon (passive/reflexive sense), and bagna (3rd-person singular present of bagnare) means here 'is lapped by / borders / is watered by'. 'de l'acqua che nel detto laco stagna' literally 'by the water that in the said lake stagnates' — de l'acqua = 'by/from the water', detto = 'aforementioned', stagna = 3rd-sing present 'stagnates' (i.e. 'that lies still in the lake').
segments.inferno-20-g69: Loco è nel mezzo là dove ... is a locative statement: 'There is a place in the middle where ...'. The enumerated nouns (''l trentino pastore', 'quel di Brescia', '’l veronese') are subjects of the following verb; 'quel di Brescia' means 'the one from Brescia'. 'segnar poria' is an archaic periphrasis: segnare (infinitive) + poria (conditional) = 'could mark/point out'.
segments.inferno-20-g70: s’e’ fesse quel cammino is an archaic conditional clause: s' here represents 'se' ('if'), and fesse is an older subjunctive/conditional form meaning 'had been made' or 'were made' (cf. modern 'fosse fatto' or 'fusse fatto'). The clause therefore means 'if that path/pathway had been made' and links back to 'poria' ('could ... if ...').
segments.inferno-20-g71: Siede is 3rd-person singular present of sedere used here intransitively to mean 'sits' or 'lies' (i.e. 'is located'). Peschiera is the place-name subject. The verb places the town geographically.
segments.inferno-20-g72: This is a descriptive noun phrase: 'bello e forte arnese'—'a beautiful and strong arnese' (arnese here = fortification/defensive work). 'da fronteggiar' is da + infinitive expressing purpose or capability ('to confront/face'); the phrase as a whole means 'a fine and strong fortification to face the Bresciani and the Bergamaschi.'
segments.inferno-20-g73: ove introduces a relative/adverbial clause 'where' and governs 'la riva ’ntorno più discese' — 'the shore all around falls/slopes down more'. '’ntorno' (intorno) is an adverb 'around', più modifies the verb (more/steeper), and discese is used to express the shore's downward slope (3rd-person singular sense 'slopes down/descends').
segments.inferno-20-g129: Ma is the simple adversative 'but'. Vienne is the 3rd-person singular present subjunctive (from venire) used here as an exhortation — roughly 'come on' or 'come now' in older/poetic usage. Omai is an adverb 'by now/now', intensifying the immediacy of the exhortation.
segments.inferno-20-g130: Ché (= perché) introduces the reason 'for/that'. Già = 'already'. Tiene is 3rd-person singular present of tenere meaning 'holds/marks' (here 'the boundary is in force/holds'). '’l confine d’amendue li emisperi' literally 'the boundary of both the hemispheres' (d’amendue = archaic di + ambe + due = of both). E tocca = 'and touches/reaches' (tocca, 3rd sg. present) governs 'l’onda' (the wave/sea). Sotto + proper names (Sobilia, Caino, e le spine) gives the place 'beneath Sobilia, Cain and the Spines'.
segments.inferno-20-g131: E 'and' links the clauses. Già iernotte = 'already last night' (iernotte = last night). Fu is passato remoto (3rd sg. of essere) 'was' — a typical poetic past. 'La luna tonda' = 'the moon round/full' (tonda used for the full moon).
segments.inferno-20-g132: Ben = 'well' or 'surely' (adverb of emphasis). Ten here is an archaic form of tenere used with de + infinitive to express obligation or strong recommendation — 'you ought/should to'. De' is the contracted preposition di before the infinitive. Ricordar is the infinitive 'to remember' (here: 'you should well remember').
segments.inferno-20-g133: Ché = 'for' introducing the reason. Non is the negation. Ti = indirect object 'you' (to/for you). Nocque is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of nocere 'to harm' (so 'it harmed'). Alcuna volta = 'at any time/ever' ('alcuna' used in a negative context = 'ever/at any time'). Per here means 'through/during', and 'la selva fonda' = 'the deep/dense wood' (fonda = related to fondo, here 'deep').
segments.inferno-20-g134: Sì here = 'so/thus' (manner adverb). Mi = indirect object 'to me'. Parlava is the imperfect (3rd sg.) of parlare, indicating continuous or repeated past action: 'he was speaking to me' or 'he spoke to me (in this way)'.
segments.inferno-20-g135: E = 'and'. Andavamo is 1st-person plural imperfect of andare ('we were going' / 'we went on'), indicating continuous movement. Introcque is an archaic adverb meaning roughly 'meanwhile' or 'in the meantime', modifying andavamo to show simultaneous/continuing action.
segments.inferno-20-g87: Lì is a locative adverb placed at the clause's start for emphasis — it means 'there' and sets the place for the following actions.
segments.inferno-20-g88: Per fuggire is a purpose phrase: per + infinitive expresses 'in order to/so as to' (purpose). Ogne is an archaic form of ogni ('every'), consorzio umano is a noun phrase 'human association' with umano qualifying consorzio.
segments.inferno-20-g89: Ristette is the (poetic/archaic) passato remoto of ristere/ristare meaning 'he remained/stayed'. Con suoi servi = 'with his servants' (con + possessive agreeing in gender/number). A far sue arti is an infinitival purpose/intent: a + infinitive = 'to (do)'; sue arti = 'his arts/skills'.
segments.inferno-20-g90: E visse links past actions with the conjunction e 'and'; visse is passato remoto of vivere ('he lived'). The comma shows a slight pause before the next coordinated clause.
segments.inferno-20-g91: E vi lasciò suo corpo vano: e = 'and'; vi is a locative pronoun/adverb 'there' referring back to the place; lasciò is passato remoto of lasciare 'he left/left behind'; suo corpo = 'his body'; vano is an attributive adjective (here 'lifeless/empty/vain' in sense) modifying corpo.
segments.inferno-20-g92: Li uomini poi = 'the men, then' (Li is the article); che ’ntorno erano sparti is a relative clause: che = 'who/that', ’ntorno (intorno) = 'around', erano sparti is an archaic/perfect participial idea meaning 'were scattered' (erano = imperfect of essere + sparti as past participle). S’accolsero a quel loco is a pronominal verb form (si + accolsero) here meaning 'they gathered/took themselves together' at that place.
segments.inferno-20-g93: Ch’era forte = 'which was strong/fortified' (ch' = che; era = imperfect of essere). Per lo pantan = 'because of/on account of the marsh' (per used causally), ch’avea da tutte parti = relative clause 'which had (extended) on all sides' (avea = imperfect of avere; da tutte parti = 'from/on all sides').
segments.inferno-20-g94: Fer is an archaic/poetic past form (from fare) meaning 'they made' or 'they built' (3rd plural past). La città sovra quell' ossa morte = 'the city upon those dead bones' (sovra = 'over/on top of'; ossa morte = 'dead bones/remains').
segments.inferno-20-g95: E per colei che ’l loco prima elesse: e = 'and'; per here has a causal/relational sense ('on account of' or 'because of'); colei = 'she who' or 'the one' (feminine demonstrative); che introduces the relative clause; ’l loco prima elesse = 'first chose the place' (prima = 'first' or 'before', elesse is passato remoto of eleggere/ scegliere 'chose').
segments.inferno-20-g96: Mantüa l’appellar sanz’ altra sorte: Mantua at the start is the name; l’appellar is an infinitival noun/phrase 'to call it' or 'the calling it' (object clitic l- = 'it' attached to appellar); sanz' (sanza) = senza 'without'; altra sorte = 'any other kind/lot' — here idiomatically 'without any other name'. Word order is inverted (name first) for emphasis.
segments.inferno-20-g97: Già is 'already'. Fuor (fuori) here is an adverb marking contrast/location; le genti sue = 'his people' (plural); dentro = 'inside/within' specifying where; più spesse = 'more thick/dense' (comparative adjective agreeing in gender/number with genti). The line uses inverted/elliptical word order typical of poetry.
segments.inferno-20-g98: Prima che introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'before'. After it, Dante uses ricevesse (imperfect subjunctive) because prima che typically governs the subjunctive for an action not yet realized in the reference timeframe. La Mattia da Casalodi = the man Mattia of the Casalodi family; da Pinamonte = 'from/by Pinamonte' (agent/source); inganno = 'deceit/treachery'; ricevesse = imperfect subjunctive of ricevere 'he received'.
segments.inferno-20-g99: 'Però' is a coordinating adverb meaning 'therefore' linking this remark to what preceded; 't’' is the clitic for 'to you' (indirect object) attached to the verb; 'assenno' is an archaic first-person present meaning 'I indicate / I tell'; 'che' introduces the content clause that follows ('that...').
segments.inferno-20-g100: This conditional clause opens with 'se' ('if') and the subject 'tu' follows; 'mai' = 'ever'. The verb 'odi' (hear) is implied in the line's word order but not listed in the provided words; 'originar' is an infinitive meaning 'to be said to originate' (i.e. 'be described as originating'); 'la mia terra' is a possessive noun phrase 'my land/country'; 'altrimenti' = 'otherwise'. The sequence expresses 'if you ever hear [it] said that my land originated otherwise.'
segments.inferno-20-g101: Syntax is inverted/elliptical: 'la verità' (the truth) is the topic; 'nulla' here functions as a negative qualifier ('no' / 'not at all') modifying 'menzogna' ('falsehood'); 'frodi' is an archaic subjunctive-form verb meaning 'may deceive' or 'deceive' — the whole clause therefore issues a prohibition/wish: 'let no falsehood deceive the truth' (i.e. do not allow lies to mislead you about the truth).
segments.inferno-20-g102: E io: is a short utterance-marking clause meaning “And I:” where E is the coordinating conjunction linking this speech act to what precedes, and io is the subject (first-person pronoun) placed after the conjunction for emphasis; the colon marks direct speech.
segments.inferno-20-g103: «Maestro, is the vocative address. Maestro is a noun used directly to name or address the speaker’s guide (Virgil); no grammatical change other than the vocative force.
segments.inferno-20-g104: i tuoi ragionamenti mi son sì certi e prendon sì mia fede: literal order is article + possessive + noun; son is the present indicative of essere (they are) and mi is an indirect-dative-like clitic meaning “to me” (so: “are to me”). prendon is plural present indicative of prendere (prendono in modern Italian) with the object mia fede; the two sì adverbs intensify (‘so … so …’). Together the clause says the master’s arguments are so convincing to me and so seize my faith/trust.
segments.inferno-20-g105: che li altri mi sarien carboni spenti: che introduces a result/consequence clause (‘that’). li altri is a poetic plural nominal group ‘the others’. sarien is an archaic conditional form (modern sarebbero) expressing a hypothetical/resultative attitude: “would be.” mi again marks the experiencer ‘to me’. carboni spenti is a noun + past participle used adjectivally: ‘(mere) extinguished coals/embers,’ a metaphor for being of no use or relevance.
segments.inferno-20-g106: Ma dimmi, is an imperative request: Ma = 'but' (softening/contrastive), and dimmi is the imperative of dire with the enclitic mi ('tell me'). The comma marks continuation into the question.
segments.inferno-20-g107: de la gente che procede is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘of the people who proceed/come forth’. de (poetic form of di) governs la gente; che introduces a relative clause whose verb procede is present indicative meaning ‘who come/are coming/originate’. Word order is normal for a noun + relative clause.
segments.inferno-20-g108: se tu ne vedi alcun degno di nota; is a conditional interrogative: se = 'if', tu is the subject, ne is the clitic pronoun referring back to 'della gente' (meaning 'of them' or 'of it'), and vedi is present indicative 'do you see.' alcun is an indefinite ('any/a certain') and degno di nota is an adjective phrase 'worthy of note/reputation'. The whole clause asks whether he sees any noteworthy person among them.
segments.inferno-20-g109: ché solo a ciò la mia mente rifiede: ché (because/for) introduces the reason; solo a ciò = 'only to that/for that alone'; la mia mente is the subject; rifiede is an archaic/poetic verb (lemma rifiedere) meaning roughly 'returns to/turns back to' or 'fixes upon.' The verb is present indicative and the phrase means 'for that alone my mind turns back/returns.'
segments.inferno-20-g110: Allor mi disse: is a simple past narration: Allor = 'then/thereupon'; mi is the indirect object clitic 'to me'; disse is passato remoto of dire 'he said'. The order is typical narrative (temporal adverb + indirect object + verb).
segments.inferno-20-g111: «Quel che da la gota porge la barba in su le spalle brune,: Quel che is a relative/head phrase 'he who' or 'that which'; da la gota porge la barba = 'from the cheek he extends/offers the beard' where porge is present indicative (porge → 'extends/throws'); in su = a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'up on/over'; le spalle brune = 'the brown shoulders.' The relative describes physical appearance; the verb porge governs the phrase 'la barba'.
segments.inferno-20-g112: fu—quando Grecia fu di maschi vòta,: fu (passato remoto of essere) states 'he was' or 'it was', followed by a temporal subordinate quando Grecia fu di maschi vòta = 'when Greece was emptied/devoid of men.' The second fu again is past 'was'; di maschi vòta uses di + plural noun and vòta (poetic/archaic for vuota) to mean 'empty of men.'
segments.inferno-20-g113: sì ch’a pena rimaser per le cune— augure,: sì ch’ = 'so that'; a pena = 'scarcely/with difficulty' (an idiom); rimaser is an archaic past form (remained; rimasero in modern Italian) meaning 'remained'; per le cune = 'for the cradles' or 'at the cradles' (cune = poetic plural of cuna, 'cradle'/'birthplace'); augure follows after the dash as the noun to which the previous clause refers ('so that scarcely augurs remained').
segments.inferno-20-g114: e diede ’l punto con Calcanta in Aulide a tagliar la prima fune.: e = 'and' connects the clause; diede is passato remoto of dare 'gave' but here means 'gave the signal' or 'set the moment'; ’l (elided article) punto = 'the moment/point' (object); con Calcanta = 'with Calchas' (Calcanta is vernacular form of Calchas); in Aulide = 'at Aulis'; a tagliar is an infinitive of purpose or result 'to cut' (poetic infinitive tagliar instead of modern tagliare); la prima fune = 'the first rope.' The construction uses diede + a + infinitive to indicate causing/ordering the action.
segments.inferno-20-g40: 'Vedi' is the 2nd-person singular imperative (addressing the reader: 'see' or 'behold') taking 'Tiresia' as its direct object; 'Tiresia' is the proper name (Tiresias) in the object position.
segments.inferno-20-g41: This relative clause begins with 'che' = 'who/that'. 'mutò' is the passato remoto (3rd sg.) of mutare, meaning 'changed'; 'sembiante' is a noun meaning 'appearance'. 'quando' is temporal 'when'; the phrase 'di maschio femmina divenne' uses 'di' poetically for 'from' and places 'maschio' (male/man) before 'femmina' (female/woman) to say 'he became from male into female' — 'divenne' is passato remoto 'became'.
segments.inferno-20-g42: 'cangiandosi' is a reflexive gerund/continuous participle ('changing himself' / 'changing'), indicating an action simultaneous to the main verb. 'le membra tutte quante' is the object phrase: definite article + 'membra' (limbs), with 'tutte quante' reinforcing 'all of them' (a pleonastic/all-inclusive expression).
segments.inferno-20-g43: 'e' is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. 'prima' here is an adverb meaning 'first' or 'previously', introducing the sequence of actions (i.e., 'first ... then ...').
segments.inferno-20-g44: 'poi' is an adverb meaning 'then'/'afterwards', continuing the temporal sequence begun by 'prima'.
segments.inferno-20-g45: 'ribatter' is the infinitive ('to strike/beat back') that functions as the complement of 'convenne'. The first 'li' before 'convenne' is an anticipatory clitic (object pronoun 'them'); 'convenne' is passato remoto used impersonally here to mean 'it was necessary' / 'he had to'. 'li duo serpenti avvolti' is the explicit noun phrase 'the two entwined serpents' (article + numeral + noun + past participle).
segments.inferno-20-g46: 'con' is the preposition 'with'; 'la' is the definite article 'the'; 'verga' is a concrete noun here meaning 'rod' or staff — the instrument used to strike the serpents.
segments.inferno-20-g47: The clause introduced by 'che' gives purpose/result: 'rïavesse' is an imperfect subjunctive form (old spelling of riavere) meaning 'might have again' / 'might regain' (used after 'che' to express the intended result). 'le maschili penne' is the object: definite article + adjective 'maschili' (masculine) modifying 'penne' (plumes/feathers) — here figuratively the male attributes regained.
segments.inferno-20-g48: 'Aronta' (Aruns) is a proper name and the grammatical subject; 'è' is the present tense 'is'; 'quel' is the demonstrative 'that one' (identifying him); 'ch' (ch') is the relative 'who/that'. 'al' is the contraction 'a + il' ('to/at the'), 'ventre' is 'belly' or 'underside', 'li' here is the article/old clitic form tied to the following reflexive 's'atterga', and 's'atterga' is a reflexive present form (archaic) meaning 'turns/leans/sets himself' (literally 'turns himself toward/against'). Together the relative clause says roughly 'who turns/leans his belly toward them'.
segments.inferno-20-g49: 'che' is a relative 'which/that'; 'ne'' (a contraction of 'in + the') here functions adverbially with 'monti' to mean 'in the mountains'; 'monti' is plural 'mountains'; 'di Luni' is the genitive/locative 'of Luni' (the place name).
segments.inferno-20-g50: 'dove' = 'where'. 'ronca' (from roncare) here is a transitive/agentive sense meaning 'hewn/cut' (i.e., to clear or quarry); 'lo Carrarese' is 'the Carrarese' (a person from Carrara); 'che' is the relative 'who'; 'di sotto' is an adverbial phrase 'beneath'/'below'; 'alberga' is present-indicative 'dwell/lodge' — the clause locates the quarrying/dwelling activity ('where the Carrarese who dwells below quarriers [work]').
segments.inferno-20-g51: 'ebbe' is passato remoto of avere ('had'); 'tra i bianchi marmi' = 'among the white marbles' (tra = among/between, 'bianchi marmi' = white marbles); 'la spelonca' = 'the cave' (direct object); 'per sua dimora' = 'for his dwelling' (per used as 'as' or 'for the purpose of'). The clause states that he 'had the cave among the white marbles as his dwelling.'
segments.inferno-20-g52: 'onde' = 'from which' / 'therefore' introducing the consequence. 'a guardar' is the infinitive of purpose 'to watch/look at'; 'le stelle' = 'the stars'; 'e 'l mar' = 'and the sea' (the elided article 'l' before 'mar'); 'non li era la veduta tronca' = 'his sight/view was not cut off' — here 'non ... era' is the imperfect of essere, 'la veduta tronca' = 'the truncated view', meaning his view toward stars and sea was not obstructed.
segments.inferno-20-g53: 'E' is 'and'. 'quella' is the demonstrative 'that one' (feminine). 'che' begins a relative clause. 'ricuopre' (from ricuoprire) is present-indicative meaning 'covers over' or 'wraps up'; 'le mammelle' is the direct object 'the breasts'.
segments.inferno-20-g54: Relative clause 'che tu non vedi' = 'which you do not see.' 'tu' is the 2nd-person subject pronoun and 'vedi' is present-indicative 'you see' (negated by 'non').
segments.inferno-20-g55: 'con' = 'with'; 'le trecce' = 'the braids/tresses' (definite article + noun); 'sciolte' is a past participle/adjectival form (agreeing with 'trecce') meaning 'loose' or 'untied' (literally 'having the braids undone').
segments.inferno-20-g56: 'e' = 'and'. 'ha' is 3rd-person singular present of avere ('has'). 'di là' is an adverbial phrase 'on the other side'/'over there'. 'ogne' (archaic 'ogna') means 'every'/'each'. 'pilosa pelle' = 'hairy skin' (adjective + noun), i.e., on that side she has a hairy hide.
segments.inferno-20-g57: 'Manto' here is a proper name (the mythic Manto) or personified figure; 'fu' is passato remoto of essere ('was'), used in narrative to identify her.
segments.inferno-20-g58: Relative clause: 'che' = 'who'; 'cercò' is passato remoto of cercare ('sought'/'searched'); 'per terre molte' = 'through many lands' (per = through, 'terre' = lands, 'molte' = many).
segments.inferno-20-g59: 'poscia' = 'afterwards/then'; 'si puose' is reflexive passato remoto of porre (si porse/si puose) meaning 'she placed/settled herself'; 'là' = 'there'; 'dove nacqu' io' = 'where I was born' (nacqu' = archaic past 'was born').
segments.inferno-20-g60: 'onde' = 'therefore'/'whence'. 'un poco' = 'a little'. 'mi piace' literally 'it pleases me' but here functions as 'I would like'; the clause introduced by 'che' takes a subjunctive: 'mascolte' = 'that you listen to me' with 'ascolte' in the present subjunctive (imperative-like polite request).
segments.inferno-20-g61: 'Poscia che' = 'after that/after', introducing a temporal clause. 'l' (the elided article) + 'padre' = 'the father'; 'suo' = 'his/her/its (own)'. 'di vita uscìo' = 'went out of life' (uscìo, passato remoto, 'died/left life'). 'e venne serva la città di Baco' = 'and the city of Bacchus became a servant' (venne used impersonally/predicatively; 'serva' here means 'subject to' or 'subjugated'). Grammatically, two coordinated clauses describe the consequence of the father's death.
segments.inferno-20-g62: 'questa' = 'this (one)'; 'gran tempo' = 'a long time' (gran = great, tempo = time); 'per lo mondo' = 'through the world'; 'gio' is an archaic/directional adverb meaning 'down'/'downwards' or 'below' — here indicating movement or wandering through the world, often in a downward sense.
segments.inferno-20-g115: Subject + passato remoto of avere + noun: 'Euripilo ebbe nome' literally 'Euripylus had a name' — a concise way to say 'he was called Euripylus.' 'ebbe' is the literary passato remoto of avere (he had).
segments.inferno-20-g116: Clause with a clitic and verb: 'e così ’l canta l’alta mia tragedìa in alcun loco' — 'and thus my lofty tragedy sings him in some place.' The clitic '’l' (lo) appears before the verb as a direct-object pronoun; 'canta' is 3rd-person singular present of cantare; 'l’alta mia tragedìa' is the noun phrase 'my lofty (high) poem' (Dante uses tragedìa in the elevated sense). 'in alcun loco' = 'in some place / in one passage.'
segments.inferno-20-g117: 'Ben lo sai tu che la sai tutta quanta.' — 'ben' (well) emphatically modifies the verb; 'lo' is the clitic object 'it' (referring back to the mention). 'sai' is 2nd-person present of sapere ('you know'); the relative clause 'che la sai tutta quanta' repeats the idea: 'that you know it entirely.' 'la' is a feminine object clitic (referring to the story/tragedy), and 'tutta quanta' is a reinforced phrase meaning 'completely / all of it.'
segments.inferno-20-g118: 'Quell’ altro che ne’ fianchi è così poco' is a relative clause describing 'that other one.' 'Quell’ altro' = 'that other (one)'; 'ne’ fianchi' is an archaic contraction of 'nei fianchi' = 'in the flanks/sides'; 'è' is present of essere and 'così poco' = 'so small/slight.'
segments.inferno-20-g119: Simple nominative identification: 'Michele Scotto fu,' — 'was Michele Scotto.' 'fu' is the passato remoto of essere (he was), typical in narrative/poetic register.
segments.inferno-20-g120: Relative clause with passato remoto: 'che veramente de le magiche frode seppe ’l gioco.' 'che' = 'who/that'; 'veramente' = 'truly'; 'de le' is an archaic spacing of 'delle' ('of the'); 'magiche frode' = 'magical deceits'; 'seppe' is passato remoto of sapere ('knew' / 'learned'), and '’l gioco' = 'the trick/game' — an idiom: 'knew the trick of magical deceits.'
segments.inferno-20-g121: 'Vedi Guido Bonatti;' is an imperative (or vivid present) addressed to the listener: 'See/Behold Guido Bonatti.' 'Vedi' here functions as a directive to notice the named figure.
segments.inferno-20-g122: Parallel imperative: 'vedi Asdente,' — 'see Asdente.' 'Asdente' is a nickname (literally 'toothless') used as a proper name; the form stands as the object of 'vedi.'
segments.inferno-20-g123: Complex clause of regret: 'ch’avere inteso al cuoio e a lo spago ora vorrebbe,' where 'ch’' = 'who,' and 'vorrebbe' is the conditional ('would want'). The infinitive perfect 'avere inteso' (to have attended/paid heed) expresses a wished-for past action: 'would now wish to have attended to/pay attention to the leather and the cord.' 'al' = 'a + il' and 'a lo' (archaic) = 'allo'; word order places the perfect infinitive before the conditional verb.
segments.inferno-20-g124: 'ma tardi si pente.' — 'but he repents late.' 'ma' = 'but'; 'tardi' adverb 'late'; 'si pente' is reflexive present ('he/one repents' or 'he feels regret for himself'). The sense is 'he repents, but too late.'
segments.inferno-20-g125: 'Vedi le triste che lasciaron l’ago,' — imperative 'see' plus the feminine plural article and adjective 'le triste' = 'the wretched (women)'; 'che lasciaron' is a relative clause with passato remoto plural ('they left'); 'l’ago' = 'the needle' (direct object).
segments.inferno-20-g126: 'la spuola e ’l fuso,' lists the tools abandoned: 'the shuttle and the spindle.' Articles 'la' and '’l' (il) precede the nouns; the nouns are technical terms of weaving/ sewing.
segments.inferno-20-g127: 'e fecersi ’ndivine;' — 'and they made themselves diviners.' 'fecersi' is the past (here infinitive/participle form representing 'fecero si' or 'fecer si') with reflexive pronoun: 'they became/made themselves'; '’ndivine' = 'divine/soothsayers' (ndivina = 'diviner').
segments.inferno-20-g128: 'fecer malie con erbe e con imago.' — 'they did spells with herbs and with an image/effigy.' 'fecer' is passato remoto plural 'they did/made'; 'malie' = 'spells, enchantments'; 'con erbe' = 'with herbs'; 'imago' is a learned/Latin loan meaning 'effigy' or 'image' used here as a magical implement.

sentences.inferno-20-s9: Why do you leave the battle?
sentences.inferno-20-s11: And there remained nothing but to fall down into the valley as far as Minos, who seizes every one.
sentences.inferno-20-s12: Look how he has made a breast of his shoulders; because he wished to see too far ahead, he looks backward and makes a retreating path.
sentences.inferno-20-s5: If God lets you, reader, take any profit from your reading, now consider for yourself how I could keep my face dry, when I saw our likeness so near and so twisted that the tears from the eyes wetted the buttocks through the cleft.
sentences.inferno-20-s6: Certainly I was weeping, leaning on one of the stumps of the hard rock, so that my guide said to me: “Are you still one of the other fools?”
sentences.inferno-20-s7: Here pity lives when it is well dead; who is more wicked than the one who brings passion into the divine judgment?
sentences.inferno-20-s8: Lift your head, lift it, and see to whom the earth opened its eyes among the Thebans; whereupon they all shouted: 'Where do you rush, Amphiaraus?'
sentences.inferno-20-s1: Because of a new suffering I must compose verses and supply subject‑matter to the twentieth canto of the first song, which is about the submerged.
sentences.inferno-20-s2: I was already entirely prepared to look down into the uncovered depth, which was being wetted with anguished weeping; and I saw people coming through the rounded valley, silent and weeping, at the pace that the forgetful keep in this world.
sentences.inferno-20-s3: As my sight sank lower among them, it wondrously appeared that each one had been overturned between the chin and the beginning of the chest; for the face had been turned back from the loins, and they had to go backward, because seeing ahead had been taken from them.
sentences.inferno-20-s4: Perhaps by force already some one was thus wholly swept away; but I did not see him, nor do I believe that it is so.
sentences.inferno-20-s21: There it is necessary that everything that cannot remain in Benaco's bosom should fall,
sentences.inferno-20-s22: As soon as the water begins to run, it is no longer called Benaco but Mencio up to Governolo, where it falls into the Po.
sentences.inferno-20-s23: It has not run far when it finds a marshy pool, in which it spreads out and makes it marshy; and in summer it is sometimes wont to be wretched.
sentences.inferno-20-s24: Thence, passing by, the harsh maiden saw land in the middle of the marsh, bare of cultivation and of inhabitants.
sentences.inferno-20-s17: Up in fair Italy there lies a lake, at the foot of the Alp that encloses Germany above the Tyrol, which is called Benaco.
sentences.inferno-20-s18: I believe it is watered by a thousand springs and more, between Garda and Val Camonica and the Pennines, by the water that stagnates in that lake.
sentences.inferno-20-s19: There is a spot in the middle where the Trentine shepherd, the one from Brescia, and the Veronese could mark it, if that path had been made.
sentences.inferno-20-s20: Peschiera sits, a fine and strong bulwark to face the Brescian and the Bergamasque, where the shore all around slopes down more.
sentences.inferno-20-s37: But come now, for already the boundary of both hemispheres holds and the wave reaches beneath Sobilia, Cain and the Spines; and already last night the moon was full: be sure to remember well, for it never harmed you at any time in the deep wood.
sentences.inferno-20-s38: So he spoke to me, and we went on meanwhile.
sentences.inferno-20-s25: There, in order to escape every human association, he stayed with his servants to practice his arts; he lived there, and there he left his lifeless body.
sentences.inferno-20-s26: The men, then, who had been scattered around gathered at that place, which was strong because of the marsh that surrounded it on all sides.
sentences.inferno-20-s27: They built the city over those dead bones; and because of the woman who first chose the place, they called it Mantua without any other name.
sentences.inferno-20-s28: Already his people were thicker inside, before Mattia of Casalodi received treachery from Pinamonte.
sentences.inferno-20-s29: So I tell you that, if you ever hear my country said to have originated otherwise, let no falsehood deceive the truth.
sentences.inferno-20-s30: And I: "Master, your reasonings are so certain to me and so win my trust, that the others would be like extinguished embers to me."
sentences.inferno-20-s31: But tell me, of the people who proceed, if you see any worthy of notice; for only to that my mind returns."
sentences.inferno-20-s32: Then he said to me: "He whose beard reaches from the cheek up over the brown shoulders was—when Greece was emptied of men—so that scarcely augurs remained for the cradles—an augur, and he gave the signal with Calchas at Aulis to cut the first rope."
sentences.inferno-20-s13: Behold Tiresias, who changed his appearance when he became a woman from a man, changing all his limbs; and, first and then, he had to strike back the two entwined serpents with the rod so that he might regain his masculine plumage.
sentences.inferno-20-s14: Aruns is the one who leans his belly toward them, who in the mountains of Luni — where the Carrarese quarrier cuts below — had among the white marbles the cave for his dwelling; hence, to watch the stars and the sea his view was not cut off.
sentences.inferno-20-s15: And the one who covers her breasts — which you do not see — with loose braids, and who on the other side has every hairy skin, was Manto, who sought through many lands; afterwards she settled herself where I was born; therefore I should like you to listen to me a little.
sentences.inferno-20-s16: After her father left life and the city of Bacchus became a servant, this one long wandered through the world downward.
sentences.inferno-20-s33: His name was Euripylus, and so my lofty tragedy mentions him in one place: you know it well — you who know the whole thing.
sentences.inferno-20-s34: That other one — who is so slight in the flanks — was Michele Scotto, who truly knew the trick of magical deceits.
sentences.inferno-20-s35: Look at Guido Bonatti; look at Asdente, who would now wish to have paid heed to the hide and the cord, but he repents too late.
sentences.inferno-20-s36: See the wretched ones who left the needle, the shuttle and the spindle, and made themselves soothsayers; they practised spells with herbs and with an image.
